entry.title: sqlany_execute() failed but sqlany_error() didnt return an error.
entry.content:
Hi, ALL,

For some reason the following code:

 

[code]

if( !m_api.sqlany_execute( m_stmt ) )
{
 char buffer[SACAPI_ERROR_SIZE + 20] {};
char sqlstate[6];
int rc;
rc = m_api.sqlany_error( m_conn, buffer, sizeof( buffer ) );
break;
}

 

does not fill the "buffer" variable with the error message.

 

Could someone please suggest what could be the issue and how to perform the successful query execution.

 

Thank you.

entry.title: monitor material coverage does not display supplier
entry.content:
Dear sap,

When we use app-monitor material coverage, it does not display supplier for some external materials.

For example, material JH5104000010  does not display supplier, But material JH5104000030 is OK.

But when we use app-Stock/Requirements List(MD04), it could display supplier with the material JH5104000010 .

And we find the only difference between these two materials is 

JH5104000030 have PO, but JH5104000010 only have PR. 

Is this the reason for the inconsistency in display between the two materials in the -monitor material coverage？

Thanks 

Ying

entry.title: Re: Chart of Depreciation created with Reference and can...
entry.content:
Hi!

Error ACC_AA116 states that there are missing parallel depreciation area for Depreciation Area 34.

In S/4HANA, for each posting in real time area, you must also have a parallel area assigned to Group Currency that adopt values from the posting area.

It is important to mention that the if you plan to use more the same Chart of Depreciation for more than one Company Code, the customizing between must be the same in regards of Depreciation Areas and Currencies. Otherwise, please consider to have different Chart of Depreciation for each Company Code.

In KBA 0003002785 we have an example on how your customizing should look like:

Transaction OADB:
Chart of Depr. XXYY

Depreciation area   1   Ledger group 0L   Accounting principle LGAP    Posting in real time
Depreciation area 31   Ledger group 0L   Accounting principle LGAP    Does not post
Depreciation area 32   Ledger group 2L   Accounting principle IFRS     Posting in real time
Depreciation area 33   Ledger group 2L   Accounting principle IFRS     Does not post
Depreciation area 42   Ledger group 4L   Accounting principle ABCD    Posting in real time
Depreciation area 43   Ledger group 4L   Accounting principle ABCD    Does not post

Transaction OABT:
Chart of Depr. XXYY                      

AreaName of Depreciation AreaCurrency Type ValAdIdAPC Ttr IdntTrm
1 Book deprec. Ledger group 0L / Accounting principle LGAP 0 0 
31Parallel Curr Area for Area 1, Ledger group 0L / Accounting principle LGAP30 Group Currency 1x1x
32Book deprec. Ledger group 2L / Accounting principle IFRS        0 0 
33Parallel Curr Area for Area 32, Ledger group 2L / Accounting principle IFRS       30 Group Currency    32x32x
42Book deprec. Ledger group 4L / Accounting principle ABCD  0 0 
43Parallel Curr Area for Area 42, Ledger group 4L / Accounting principle ABCD30 Group Currency    42x42x

Once customizing is in order, the error should be resolved.

entry.title: Inbound delivery deleted in ERP but available in EWM
entry.content:
The system is S/4 HANA 2021 version.  There is a inbound delivery is created against a PO and distributed to EWM. Then it is been processed in EWM through PRDI or Run inbound process. Then there is a queue error for the inbound delivery after the processing which shows as "Notified quantity exceeds quantity.......". When the PO is checked the purchase order history, the GRN is already reflected for the full quantity. Then to check it in ERP, in VL33n, the delivery is shown as "Archived or deleted". There is another IBD which holds the GRN for the same quantity.

The above is the scenario when the incident is reported as all the issue was against the standard behaviour. The user deleted the IBD which is processed in EWM and where the delivery is stuck in queue, but yet created a new Inbound delivery and processed it, where the stock is reflected.

In ERP stock check, the quantity is doubled i EWM, and ERP stays normal. Even the physical and ERP stock is matched, so only in EWM the stock is inconsistent. Taking this as a reference, tried to recreate a test case in quality system, but the deletion process is cant be done. There are certain questions with the scenarios:

1)What could have been happened in first place, as in which hierarchy the process taken place to achieve the scenario?
Maybe the user could have done the GRN, then without knowing it he tried again the process. Then finds out he is wrong after the processing the delivery, so there he is deleting it, even in table the delivery is deleted.

2)How system allowed to delete a EWM distributed delivery? 
3)Against the delivered quantity in PO, how system allowed to create a IBD? 
4)How it again allowed to distributed in EWM?

Here, the scenario maybe little confusing, but the business demands a fast paced solution. Will be happy to share more details if needed.

entry.title: Re: SAC/DSP to connect with SAP ECC (underlying oracle da...
entry.content:
Hi @sumitsamal123 

Yes, it is possible to connect SAP ECC → SAP Datasphere → SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) and build real-time or near real-time analytics.

Recommended Approach

  1. Verify SAP ECC Prerequisites

    • Ensure your SAP ECC system meets the minimum DMIS (Data Migration Server) version supported by SAP Datasphere.
    • For example, SAP generally requires a supported DMIS release (such as DMIS 2018 SP level6 or higher depending on the scenario and SAP support matrix).
    • Also ensure that the SLT component is available, either:
      • Embedded within the ECC system, or
      • Installed on a dedicated SLT server (if its not feasible to upgrade the DMIS of ECC, then you upgrade the DMIS of SLT box matching the DMIS requirements for  datasphere connectivity.)

  2. Configure SLT Replication (either slt embedded in ECC or standalone, below steps are same)

    • SLT acts as the data replication framework between ECC and SAP Datasphere.
    • In transaction LTRC, create a Mass Transfer ID (MTID) and configure the source ECC connection, and make sure to select RMS during creation of MTID
    • SLT uses database triggers and logging tables to capture changes for delta replication.

  3. Connect ECC to SAP Datasphere

    • Create the source system connection in Datasphere using the SLT based integration approach.
    • For ECC systems, it is often recommended to perform direct table replication 
    • Data to be ingested to Datasphere

  4. Initial Load and Delta Replication

    • Perform the initial load from ECC to Datasphere.
    • Once completed, SLT automatically captures incremental changes through delta mechanisms.
    • New records and updates are continuously replicated to Datasphere based on the configured replication schedule.

  5. Connect SAP Datasphere to SAP Analytics Cloud

    • Establish a Live Connection between SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP Datasphere.
    • Build stories, dashboards, and analytical models in SAC on top of Datasphere datasets.
    • As new delta records arrive in Datasphere, the latest data becomes immediately available for reporting and visualization in SAC.

Suggest raising a ticket with SAP to get on the DMIS requirement.

 

Regards,

Bheemesh

entry.title: WPT Is Not Just Configuration — It Is Warehouse Movement Intelligence
entry.content:
Warehouse Process Type Is Not Just Configuration — It Is the Execution Intelligence Behind Every Warehouse Task

Manyat Thinking: Simplicity is the Ultimate Sophistication

Warehouse Process Type Is Not Just Configuration — It Is the Execution Intelligence Behind Every Warehouse Task

Warehouse Process Type Is More Than Configuration: A Different Way to Think About SAP EWM

Introduction

Whenever we learn SAP EWM, Warehouse Process Type (WPT) is usually introduced with a familiar definition:

"A configuration object used to control Warehouse Task creation and execution."

There is nothing technically wrong with this explanation. However, one question always stayed in my mind:

If WPT is only a configuration object, why does almost every warehouse movement depend on it?

That question completely changed the way I think about Warehouse Process Types. Today, I no longer see WPT as just another configuration object. Instead, I see it as the point where a warehouse movement begins to receive its execution behavior.

A Warehouse Task Can Execute… But Can It Decide?

A Warehouse Task tells an operator what to move, where to move it, and how much inventory should be moved.

However, can a Warehouse Task decide whether that movement represents Putaway, Picking, Replenishment, or an Internal Movement?

The answer is no.

That decision already exists before the Warehouse Task is created. The Warehouse Task simply executes the movement, while the Warehouse Process Type determines the business nature of that execution.

That is the difference.

Every Movement Looks Similar Until Business Gets Involved

From a purely technical perspective, every Warehouse Task moves inventory from one place to another.

From a business perspective, however, every movement serves a completely different objective.

A Putaway movement organizes warehouse storage.

A Picking movement fulfills customer demand.

A Replenishment movement protects the picking process.

An Internal Movement improves warehouse operations.

Although the physical movement may appear identical, the business purpose behind each movement is completely different. That business purpose is what changes the expected behavior of the movement, and this is where Warehouse Process Type becomes important.

Think About a Chess Game

The analogy that helped me understand this concept came from chess.

Every chess piece moves across the same board, yet nobody expects a Pawn to behave like a Queen. The board remains the same and the destination may even be identical, but the movement behavior is fundamentally different.

Warehouses operate in a very similar way.

Every Warehouse Task moves inventory, but a Putaway task should never behave like a Picking task. Likewise, a Replenishment task should never behave like an Internal Movement.

The inventory may be identical. The operator may be identical. Even the forklift may be identical.

What changes is the expected behavior of the movement.

That behavior begins with the Warehouse Process Type.

Does WPT Work Alone?

No.

This is one of the biggest misunderstandings people often have when discussing Warehouse Process Types.

I am not suggesting that WPT replaces Storage Type Search, Queue Determination, WOCR, POSC, Wave Management, or any other SAP EWM control object. Every one of these components has its own responsibility within warehouse execution.

However, before SAP EWM can apply all of those controls, the system must first understand one fundamental question:

What kind of warehouse movement is this?

That execution identity originates from the Warehouse Process Type.

The remaining EWM components then refine, optimize, and control the execution of that movement.

Why Should Business Care?

Many warehouse problems are solved by introducing additional configuration.

Sometimes, however, the actual issue is much simpler.

The movement behavior itself was never designed correctly.

A well-designed Warehouse Process Type contributes to cleaner execution, greater operational consistency, easier troubleshooting, improved resource utilization, and lower operational confusion.

The objective is not to create more Warehouse Tasks.

The objective is to create better warehouse behavior.

WPT Also Tells a Story

One interesting observation is that Warehouse Process Types do more than control execution.

They also reveal warehouse behavior.

For example, frequent Emergency Replenishment often indicates weaknesses in inventory planning.

An unusually high number of Internal Movements may suggest poor slotting decisions or warehouse imbalance.

Repeated exception tasks frequently expose unstable business processes.

Warehouse Tasks explain what happened.

Warehouse Process Types often help explain why it keeps happening.

This is where operational visibility begins to generate business value.

Looking Ahead

Warehouses continue to evolve through Robotics, Automation, MFS, and Artificial Intelligence.

All of these technologies are capable of moving inventory.

However, moving inventory is not the difficult part.

Understanding how each movement should behave is.

Automation without well-defined movement behavior simply allows mistakes to happen faster.

Good warehouse execution begins long before a forklift starts moving.

It begins when warehouse movements are designed correctly.

Conclusion

Warehouse Tasks move inventory, but movement alone never creates an efficient warehouse.

Efficient warehouses depend on predictable movement behavior.

Storage Types, Queues, WOCR, POSC, and other SAP EWM components all contribute to warehouse execution. However, before those controls can be applied, Warehouse Process Type establishes the execution identity of the movement.

That is why I no longer see WPT as just another configuration object.

I see it as the point where warehouse movement begins to receive its execution behavior.

Manyat Thinking

Warehouse Tasks execute movement.

Warehouse Process Types define movement behavior.

The goal is not to move inventory faster.

The goal is to ensure every movement behaves correctly before execution begins.

— Rahul Kumar

entry.title: Introducing API Rate Limits for SAP SuccessFactors: What You Need to Know
entry.content:
SAP has announced SAP API Policy and SAP SuccessFactors is introducing API rate limiting to improve stability, reliability, and performance for all customers and partners.

What is changing?

Soft rate limits are being applied to all existing customers. API requests will still be executed, but standardized rate limit headers will be returned in the API response header.

Hard rate limits will be enforced for all new public APIs from H2 2026 (November 13, 2026) and for all new tenants created on January 1, 2027 and after. API requests that exceed a hard rate limit will be rejected with a 429 error response code.

The following soft rate limits will be in effect in 2608 monthly release (August 17, 2026):

API Rate Limit
Tenant Level rate limit: global rate limit for all API requests across the tenant. Applies to all endpoints and operations.600 requests/minute
/odata/v2/$metadata25 requests/minute
/odata/v2/$batch25 requests/minute
/odata/v2/upsert25 requests/minute
/sfapi/v1/soap75 requests/minute
/odata/v2/FormContent25 requests/minute
/odata/v2/FormHeader50 requests/minute
/odata/v2/Goal50 requests/minute
/odata/v2/NominationTarget50 requests/minute
/odata/v2/TalentRatings25 requests/minute; Quota: 1,250/hour
/odata/v2/Candidate50 requests/minute
/odata/v2/CandidateLight50 requests/minute
/odata/v2/JobApplication75 requests/minute
/odata/v2/JobApplicationInterview25 requests/minute
/odata/v2/JobApplicationOnboardingData25 requests/minute
/odata/v2/JobApplicationQuestionResponse25 requests/minute
/odata/v2/JobApplicationStatus25 requests/minute
/odata/v2/JobApplicationStatusAuditTrail25 requests/minute
/odata/v2/JobOffer25 requests/minute
/odata/v2/JobOfferApprover25 requests/minute
/odata/v2/JobReqFwdCandidates25 requests/minute
/odata/v2/JobReqScreeningQuestion25 requests/minute
/odata/v2/JobRequisition50 requests/minute
/odata/v2/JobRequisitionLocale25 requests/minute
/odata/v2/JobRequisitionOperator25 requests/minute
/odata/v2/JobRequisitionPosting25 requests/minute
/odata/v2/OfferLetter25 requests/minute

Why are we making these changes?

SAP has introduced a new API Policy to ensure platform stability, reliability, and fair usage across all customers. Adopting this policy, SuccessFactors is implementing standardized rate limits to protect the platform from abusive traffic patterns and ensure consistent performance for all users.

What improvements can be expected?

  • Improved server stability and reliability for all customers.
  • Protection against abusive traffic patterns that degrade performance.
  • Standardized rate limit headers in API response headers will notify customers before hard limits are enforced, giving time to adjust usage.

How will customers be affected?

  • Existing customers: Soft rate limits will be in place, and you will receive the standardized rate limit headers in the API response header in 2608 monthly release. Detailed information will be posted on WNV in 2608 monthly release. No API requests in your integrations will be blocked at this stage. Some APIs already had hard rate limits in place for some tenants, and those hard rate limits will continue to remain in effect. In the future, when we convert all soft rate limits to hard rate limits, we will standardize the thresholds across all rate limits to keep consistence.
  • New customers (from January 1, 2027): Hard rate limits will apply from the start.
  • New APIs (H2 2026 onwards): All new public APIs will have hard rate limits enforced from launch.
  • Customers whose applications exceed rate limits may receive a 429 error once hard limits are enforced.

Next Steps / Customer Action Items

We understand that you may have questions and concerns, and we are here to support you through this transition. Here are the next steps:

  • Action Item 1 – Review your current API usage and reduce your API usage against the rate limit thresholds listed above after August 17, 2026, when soft rate limit headers will appear in API response headers. We will convert all soft rate limits to hard rate limits for all customers in the future and communicate in advance with detailed information and timelines.
  • Action Item 2 – Monitor the standardized rate limit headers in API response headers starting with the 2608 monthly release (August 17, 2026). Detailed information will be posted on WNV.
  • Action Item 3 – Prepare for hard rate limits for any new APIs you are developing (H2 2026) and for new tenants provisioned on or after January 1, 2027.
  • Action Item 4 – Handle 429 errors in your customer applications (e.g., BTP applications) by implementing appropriate retry logic and backoff strategies.

Have A Question?

For questions regarding this feature, please leave a comment below.

Need Support?

If you encounter any issues, please log a case to the relevant component.

entry.title: SWF_WORKFLOW_CONDITION_DEF - Workflow parameter input field disappears when property is assigned
entry.content:
HI @C_SAP_ForumUser,

Thank you for your suggestion. I implemented the solution exactly as recommended using both extension points:

  • SWF_WORKFLOW_CONDITION_DEF
  • SWF_WORKFLOW_CONDITION_EVAL

The custom workflow condition is successfully displayed in Manage Workflows for Purchase Requisitions.

However, I am facing an issue while trying to provide F4 Value Help for the custom parameter.

What I have tested

1. Without OData configuration

Parameter contains only:

  • id
  • name
  • shorttext
  • xsd_type
  • mandatory
  • ddic_type

Result

  • Input field is displayed.
  • No F4 help is available

2. Using a Custom CDS View (Scenario = Value Help)

I created a custom CDS View based on I_PurchaseRequisitionType and published it successfully.

Configured in SWF_WORKFLOW_CONDITION_DEF:

service_path
entity
property

Result

  • The parameter input field disappears completely.

3. Using SAP Standard Workflow Value Help

Instead of the custom CDS, I used the standard workflow service:

  • Service: S_MMPURWORKFLOWVH_CDS
  • EntitySet: C_PurReqnTypeForWorkflow
  • Property: PurchasingDocumentType

I verified from the $metadata that:

  • Service exists
  • EntitySet exists
  • Property exists

However, after assigning the property in the parameter definition, the workflow UI no longer displays the input field.

Summary of my observations

Configuration Result
id + name + mandatory Input field displayed
+ ddic_typeInput field displayed
+ service_pathInput field displayed
+ entity Input field displayed
+ propertyInput field disappears completely

It appears that the Workflow framework rejects the parameter once the property is specified.

My Question

Has anyone successfully implemented an F4 Value Help for a custom workflow condition parameter in SWF_WORKFLOW_CONDITION_DEF?

If yes, could you please share:

  1. Whether a standard or custom OData service was used?
  2. Does the property require additional ValueList/Common.ValueList annotations?
  3. Is F4 Help currently supported for custom workflow condition parameters in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, or is this a framework limitation?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you!

entry.title: Consuming SAP CPI Keystore Entries via Security Content OData API – Part 1
entry.content:
Introduction

I would like to explore how to access and consume Security Content information from SAP Cloud Integration (CI) using the CI Security Content OData API.

In Part 1, I'll configure the required API access, generate OAuth credentials, and test the Security Content API using Postman. "The goal of this first part is simply to establish secure access to the Security Content API. Once the API call works in Postman, the same configuration can be reused inside a CPI integration flow."

In Part 2, I'll build an SAP Cloud Integration iFlow that consumes the API, processes the response, and extracts keystore information for monitoring and reporting scenarios.

These APIs are useful for automating certificate inventory, monitoring certificate expiry, generating reports, or integrating security metadata with external monitoring tools.

Prerequisites: Before getting started, ensure that:

  • You have access to an SAP Integration Suite tenant.
  • You have permissions to create service instances and service keys in SAP BTP.
  • Postman is installed for API testing.

Locate the Security Content OData API

SAP Cloud Integration exposes Security Content information through a dedicated OData API. This API provides access to security artifacts such as:

  • Keystore Entries
  • Certificates
  • Key Pairs
  • User Credentials
  • OAuth Credentials

Navigate to the SAP API Business Hub and search for the Security Content API. Review the available resources and operations to understand the information exposed by the service. You can download the api collection file (OpenAPI JSON) and this can be imported directly into Postman.
URL for API Hub: https://api.sap.com/api/SecurityContent/overview

Create an API Instance

To consume the Security Content API, an API service instance must be available in your SAP BTP subaccount. If an API instance has not already been created:

  • Navigate to your SAP BTP subaccount.
  • Open the service instances section.
  • Create a new instance for the Cloud Integration API service.
  • Complete the instance creation wizard.

This API instance provides secure access to the Cloud Integration APIs using OAuth 2.0 client credentials. And when creating the API instance, ensure the service instance has the required authorization. This role is required to read monitoring and security metadata from the tenant.

Required role:  MonitoringDataRead

Why this matters: Without the proper role, requests to the Security Content API may return authorization errors such as 403 Forbidden.

Create a Service Key

After the API instance is created, generate a Service Key. The service key contains the OAuth credentials required by external clients such as Postman or SAP Cloud Integration to authenticate against the API.

It contains all information needed for OAuth authentication, including:

  • OAuth token URL
  • Client ID
  • Client Secret
  • API endpoint URL (Base URL for the OData API)

Test the API Using Postman

Import the Postman Collection called Security Content downloaded from Step1.

Add OAuth 2.0 Authentication

Click Get New Access Token, authenticate using the client credentials, and save the generated token at the collection level so that all requests in the collection automatically inherit it.

In the collection settings:

  • Auth Type: OAuth 2.0
  • Grant Type: Client Credentials
  • Access Token URL: Token URL from the service key
  • Client ID: Client ID from the service key
  • Client Secret: Client Secret from the service key

Update Collection Variables

Update the existing baseUrl collection variable with the API endpoint from the service key. This will makes requests reusable across environments and tenants.
baseUrl  https://<ci-api-host>/api/v1

Test the Keystore Entries Request: 

Open Keystore Entries request from the collections.
Authorization: Open the Authorization tab and select-  "inherit auth from parent". This reuses the OAuth token configured at the collection level.
Endpoint: {{baseUrl}}/KeystoreEntries?$select=Alias,Status,Type,ValidNotAfter,KeyType

Execute the Request: 

By default, the API returns an json OData response. you can request Atom/XML by including the appropriate Accept header as "application/atom+xml" If preferred.

Example fields include: Alias, KeyType, ValidNotAfter, Status.

Lessons Learned:

  • Created an independent API instance for accessing SAP CI OData services as needed.
  • Assigned the required MonitoringDataRead permission.
  • Generated a service key, Configured and verified OAuth 2.0 authentication in Postman and can be used in CI as is smoothly.
  • Verified that the API's are accessible using OAuth 2.0 and able to receive the data in XML or Json
What can be better:
  • what I notice is that the OData couldn't able to filter the records using "ValidNotAfter" fields, it could be very useful if it works so that I do not have to use extra functions to filter the data & the same time reducing the amount records into CI Runtime, means smaller payloads.

entry.title: VA14L displays multiple lines for each order
entry.content:
There seems to have been a change in the layout of transaction VA14L, which makes the use of the transaction a lot less clear than before. VA14L now displays multiple lines for each order.
Where before you would see just 1 line for an order. 
Please see attached examples of how it was before (1 line), and how it is now (many lines).

The variants etc. are fine and there is no recent change. 

What has changed here and why? And is it possible to reverse this change?  We would like to see just 1 line for an order. 

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: SAP for Me システム登録情報の主要ソフトウェア製品変更
entry.content:
SAP for Meに登録済みのシステム情報について、主要ソフトウェア製品（Primary Software Product）の変更方法をご教示いただけますでしょうか。

## 対象システム
SID：*
システム番号: CREDIT_CARD_NUMBER86
インストール番号：DIGITS

## 背景
当該システムは、もともとSAP ERP 6.0として運用しておりましたが、
その後SAP S/4HANA DIGITSへコンバージョンしております。
上記製品変更に加え、基盤クラウド・ハードウェアの変更もあったためSAP for Me上で既存エントリとは別にシステム新規登録しましたが、
登録時の設定誤りにより、主要ソフトウェア製品を従来の「SAP ERP 6.0」のまま登録してしまいました。

現在、実際のシステムはSAP S/4HANA DIGITSとして稼働しておりますが、SAP for Me上の登録情報のみ「SAP ERP 6.0」となっている状況です。

## 確認した内容
SAP for Me上のシステム編集画面から変更を試みましたが、以下の制約により変更できませんでした。
  - 現在の登録が「SAP ERP 6.0」の場合、変更先として「SAP ERP 6.0」または「SAP R/3」のみ選択可能
    「SAP S/4HANA」関連製品は選択肢として表示されない
  - 「SAP S/4HANA」として登録済みのシステムについては、S/4HANAの各バージョンへの変更が可能

## 変更希望内容
現在：
  Primary Software Product = SAP ERP 6.0
変更後：
  Primary Software Product = SAP S/4HANA DIGITS

## 質問事項
SAP for Meに登録済みのシステムについて、上記の通り変更する方法についてご教示いただけますでしょうか。
また、ユーザー側で変更できない仕様の場合は、以下について併せてご教示いただければ幸いです。
  - SAPにて登録情報の修正対応が可能か
  - システム再登録等の対応が必要か
  - 必要な申請手順や提供情報


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Manage Master Data in SAP Self-Billing Cockpit from Excel templates
entry.content:
• Manage Master Data in SAP Self-Billing Cockpit
  • 1. What is Manage Master Data?
  • Who should use this app?
  • 2. Navigating the Main Screen
  • Understanding the Columns
  • 3. Master Data Objects at a Glance
  • Step 1 — Download the Excel Template
  • Step 2 — Fill in Your Data
  • General rules for filling the template
  • Step 3 — Upload Your File
  • Upload Dialog — Billing Type (no destination required)
  • Upload Dialog — Company Code (Destination is required)
  • How to complete the upload
  • 7. Understanding the Two Upload Operations
  • 8. Multi-Destination Categories
  • What is a “Destination”?
  • 9. Application Logs
  • 10. Frequently Asked Questions
  • Quick Summary — The 4-Step Process

Manage Master Data in SAP Self-Billing Cockpit

1. What is Manage Master Data?

Manage Master Data is a dedicated Fiori application within SAP Self-Billing Cockpit (SBC) that gives you full control over all master data categories required for self-billing document processing without relying on the automatic daily synchronization from SAP S/4HANA, which is deprecated after the 2608 release of SBC.

Before self-billing documents can be processed or simulated, SBC needs a set of reference data: which Sold-To Parties are relevant, which Company Codes are in scope, which Tax Codes apply, and so on. This application lets you manage all of that through simple Excel file uploads and downloads.

Why does this matter?

If master data is missing, simulation will fail with errors like “Sold-To Party Not Found”, “Tax Number Mismatch”, or “UoM Mismatch”. Keeping your master data up to date in this app prevents those errors.

Who should use this app?

This application is designed for:

  • Customers with restricted access to self-billing manage master data SBC role is required for the Manage Master Data apps (SAP_SELFB_CLERK )
  • Multi-backend customers (e.g., organisations with multiple S/4HANA systems) who need to manage data per destination independently.

2. Navigating the Main Screen

When you open the Manage Master Data tile from the SBC Fiori Launchpad, you land on the main list screen. This screen shows all master data categories in a single table, giving you an instant overview of what has been uploaded and what still needs attention.

Main Screen List Page “Items” table

 

Understanding the Columns

Column What it tells you

ObjectThe master data category name (e.g., Billing Type, Company Code).
File NameThe name of the last uploaded Excel file. Shows “—” if no file has been uploaded yet.
StatusUploaded last upload successful   New no data yet   Error last upload had errors.
Total RecordsNumber of records currently stored for this category.
Records with ErrorsNumber of rows that failed validation in the last upload.
Uploaded On/AtTimestamp of the most recent upload.
Upload buttonOpens the Upload File dialog for this category.
Download buttonLets you download the current data or the blank template.
View Application LogsOpens the application log for categories with S/4HANA validation (Company Code, Order Reason).

3. Master Data Objects at a Glance

The application manages 12 standard master data categories (plus Sold-To Party Config and Sender Config for advanced scenarios). Here is a quick reference for each:

Category S/4 Validation Multi-Destination Mandatory

1Sold-To PartyDeferredYesYes
2Sales OrganisationYesNoNo
3Order ReasonYes — S/4 checkNoYes
4Company CodeYes — S/4 checkYesYes
5ProductYesNoNo
6Unit of MeasureNoNoYes
7PlantNoNoNo
8Foreign VAT CodeNoYesYes
9Billing TypeNoNoYes
10Sales Document TypeNoNoYes
11Condition TypeNoNoYes
12Tax CodeNoNoYes
What does “S/4 Validation” mean?

S/4 Validation means the system checks whether the uploaded records actually exist in your connected SAP S/4HANA system before saving them. Categories marked “Deferred” (like Sold-To Party) are validated later when you create a Sold-To Party Configuration not at upload time.

Step 1 — Download the Excel Template

Before uploading data, you need the correct Excel template for each category. Templates define the exact column structure that the system expects.

Never create your own columns or change column headers the upload will fail if the structure does not match.

  1. Find the category rowOn the main screen, locate the master data category you want to work with (e.g., Billing Type, Tax Code, Sold-To Party).
  2. Click the Download buttonClick the Download button on the right side of the row. A dropdown appears with two options.
  3. Select “Download Template”Choose Download Template to get a blank Excel file with the correct column headers pre-filled. This is your starting point for data entry.
Tip

You can also select Download Current Data to export all records currently stored in SBC for that category. This is useful for reviewing what is already there, making corrections, and re-uploading.

 Step 2 — Fill in Your Data

Open the downloaded template in Microsoft Excel (or any compatible spreadsheet tool). The first row contains the column headers — do not modify these. Enter your data starting from row 2.

General rules for filling the template

Rule Details

✅Keep column headers unchangedThe system matches columns by name. Renaming or deleting headers will cause the upload to fail.
✅Fill all mandatory fieldsMandatory fields are marked in the template. Rows with missing mandatory values will be rejected.
✅Use correct data typesNumeric fields must contain numbers; date fields must use the format specified in the template.
✅Avoid duplicate keysEach row must have a unique primary key. Duplicates within the file will be flagged during upload.
⛔Do not leave the file emptyUploading an empty file (no data rows) in Add Records mode will be rejected by the system.
⛔Do not add extra sheetsOnly the first sheet of the Excel file is processed. Additional sheets are ignored.
For Order Reason, Company Code, and other S/4-validated objects

The values you enter must exist in your connected S/4HANA system. The upload validates each row against S/4HANA and rejects any rows where the value is not found (see the S/4 Validation column in the table above). Make sure your data matches the S/4HANA configuration exactly.

Step 3 — Upload Your File

Once your Excel file is ready, click the Upload button next to the relevant category. The Upload File dialog opens.

Upload Dialog — Billing Type (no destination required)

For categories like Billing Type, Tax Code, Condition Type, and others that are not destination-specific, the dialog is straightforward:

Upload dialog for a non-destination category (Billing Type)

 

Upload Dialog — Company Code (Destination is required)

For multi-destination categories such as Company Code, Sold-To Party, and Foreign VAT Code, an additional Destination dropdown appears. You must select the target S/4HANA system before uploading:

Upload dialog for a multi-destination category (Company Code)

 

How to complete the upload

  1. Click “Browse…” and select your fileNavigate to your prepared Excel file and select it. The file name will appear in the field.
  2. Select the Destination (if shown)For multi-destination categories, choose the correct S/4HANA destination from the dropdown. This determines which backend system the data belongs to.
  3. Choose the OperationSelect Add Records to insert new data, or Delete Records to remove records matching the keys in your file.
  4. Click “Upload”The Upload button becomes active once a file is selected (and a destination is chosen, if required). Click it to start the upload process.

7. Understanding the Two Upload Operations

The Upload File dialog offers two operations. Choosing the right one is important to avoid unintended data changes.

Operation What it does When to use

➕Add RecordsAdds new records from your file. If a record with the same key already exists in SBC, it will be overridden; otherwise a new record is created.Initial loads and incremental additions.
🗑️ Delete RecordsRemoves records whose keys match the rows in your file. Only key columns need to be filled. You will see an error in the error record column if records are not found.Removing specific entries from SBC.
⚠️Delete Records is irreversible

Once records are deleted, they cannot be recovered automatically. Always download the current data first as a backup before performing a delete operation.

Best practice for a full refresh

To completely replace all data for a category, first upload a file with Delete Records containing all existing keys, then upload your new data with Add Records. This gives you full control over what is removed and what is added.

8. Multi-Destination Categories

What is a “Destination”?

A destination is a configured connection to a specific SAP S/4HANA backend system in your landscape. If your organization operates multiple S/4HANA systems (for example, one for Germany and one for the USA), each system is registered as a separate destination in SAP BTP.

The following categories are destination-aware — meaning data is stored and managed separately per S/4HANA system:

  • Sold-To Party
  • Company Code
  • Foreign VAT Code

When you upload data for these categories, you must select the destination in the Upload File dialog. This ensures that data from one S/4HANA system does not interfere with data from another.

Example

If you upload 50 Company Codes for destination S4H_PROD_DE and then upload 30 Company Codes for destination S4H_PROD_US, SBC stores them completely separately. Deleting records for S4H_PROD_DE will never affect the records for S4H_PROD_US.

Important

The Destination dropdown is mandatory for multi-destination categories. The Upload button will remain disabled until you select a destination. If you do not see any destinations in the dropdown, contact your system administrator to verify that the BTP Destination Service is correctly configured.

9. Application Logs

You can see all the S/4-related validations in the application logs (both success and error records).

10. Frequently Asked Questions

Q. My upload failed with “File format not supported”. What should I do?

Only .xlsx files (Excel 2007 and later) are supported. Make sure you are not uploading a .xls, .csv, or .ods file. Re-save your file as .xlsx in Excel and try again.

Q. The “Destination” dropdown is empty. What does this mean?

The Destination dropdown is populated from the SAP BTP Destination Service. If it is empty, no S/4HANA destinations have been configured for your tenant. Contact your system administrator to set up the BTP destinations for your S/4HANA backend systems.

Q. Some rows in my Order Reason upload were rejected with “not found in S/4HANA”. How do I fix this?

The system validates Order Reason values against your connected S/4HANA system. The rejected values do not exist in S/4HANA. Either correct the values in your Excel file to match the S/4HANA configuration, or ask your S/4HANA administrator to create the missing Order Reasons in S/4HANA first. Then re-upload only the corrected rows using Add Records.

Q. I accidentally deleted records. Can I recover them?

Deleted records cannot be automatically recovered. However, if you downloaded the current data before deleting (best practice), you can simply re-upload that file using Add Records to restore the records.

Q. I uploaded data for Company Code with destination “S4H_PROD_DE” but I cannot see it when I select “S4H_PROD_US”. Is this a bug?

This is by design. Data for multi-destination categories is strictly isolated per destination. Records uploaded for S4H_PROD_DE are only visible and usable when that destination is selected. You need to upload a separate file for S4H_PROD_US if you need the same data there.

Q. I see “View Application Logs” for some categories but not others. Why?

The View Application Logs link is shown for categories that perform S/4HANA validation during upload (currently Order Reason and Company Code). For categories without S/4HANA validation, there are no S/4-specific log entries to display, so the link is not shown.

Q. What is the difference between “Manage Master Data” and the upload tab in “Define General Parameters”?

The Manage Master Data app is the new, unified application for all master data categories. The upload tab in Define General Parameters is the older approach that only covered a subset of categories. The new app replaces it with a better user experience, more categories, multi-destination support, and per-category authorization. The old tab is deprecated and will be removed in a future release.

Quick Summary — The 4-Step Process

  1. Step 1: Download the Excel template for your category
  2. Step 2: Fill in your data following the template structure
  3. Step 3: Click Upload, select your file (and destination if required), choose Add or Delete Records

entry.title: Vendor Quota Percentage Not Available in Released CDS View I_PURGQUOTAARRGMTITEMAPI01
entry.content:
We are developing a custom report in Public Cloud to compare Quota Arrangement with Actual Procurement (Goods Receipt) and are using CDS I_PURGQUOTAARRGMTITEMAPI01 to retrieve Quota Arrangement data. However, it does not expose the Vendor Quota Percentage (for example, 41.67%) that is displayed in the Manage Quota Arrangement. The Quota Percentage is not available in any released CDS View or API.

Our report requires the Quota Percentage to calculate: Target Quantity = Total Goods Receipt Quantity × Vendor Quota Percentage.

Is the recommended approach to derive the percentage using: Vendor Quota ÷ Sum of all Vendor Quotas × 100?

Is this the same logic used by the Manage Quota Arrangement to display the Percentage?

Kindly confirm the recommended standard approach for custom development.

entry.title: Re: I want to interface market rates from Hungary Central...
entry.content:
Hi  

Short answer: There is no direct standard API to POST rates straight into S/4HANA Public Cloud, and the Hungarian Central Bank (MNB) is not a built-in Market Rates Management data provider. The two SAP-documented paths are: file upload via the Import Foreign Exchange Rates app (simplest, without fee), or the automated route via SAP Market Rates Management (MRM) using Bring Your Own Rates (BYOR) — you fetch the MNB feed yourself, push it into MRM, and MRM distributes to S/4HANA (paid).

What SAP documents for loading exchange rates

Public Cloud offers four standard methods: manual entry in the Currency Exchange Rates app; file upload via the Import Foreign Exchange Rates app; import via API using the SAP Market Rates Management service; and direct pull from an external data provider through MRM. Reference: Import Foreign Exchange Rates – SAP Help Portal and Currency Exchange Rates – SAP Help Portal.

Note: MNB is not a standard MRM provider. MRM ships with connectors for providers like the European Central Bank and LSEG/Refinitiv, not MNB. So you cannot simply "subscribe" to MNB — you have to bring the data in yourself. MNB does publish official HUF rates via a public SOAP web service, which you consume on your side.

Recommended automated path (Bring Your Own Rates)

With an MRM subscription. BYOR lets you upload rates from any source into MRM, which then distributes them to S/4HANA. In the Parallel Line — outside S/4HANA — you build an integration flow (typically in SAP Integration Suite) that calls the MNB SOAP service, maps the response to the MRM BYOR payload (fields such as Provider Code, Market Data Category = 01 for exchange rates, currency pair, rate, date), and sends a POST to the MRM BYOR API. Reference: Bring Your Own Rates to SAP Market Rates Management in 3 easy steps and MRM Blog 2: Building an Integration Flow with SAP Integration Suite.

In the Main Line — inside S/4HANA — you connect to MRM through communication scenario SAP_COM_0203 (SAP Treasury Market Rate Integration) and schedule the pull with a job in the Application Jobs app using the market-data / "Request Current Market Data" template (e.g. daily), which fills the exchange rate table automatically. Reference: How to setup S/4HANA Cloud to get market rates automatically and MRM Blog 1: Setting up MRM in SAP BTP.

Verify: BYOR requires the SAP Market Rates Management service to be subscribed and entitled in your BTP subaccount (Basic/Advanced plan) — this is a separate subscription/licence, not included by default. Reference: Market Rates, Bring Your Own Rates – SAP Discovery Center.

Simplest alternative (no BTP/MRM subscription)

If you don't want the BTP/MRM setup, download MNB rates, prepare the file in the required layout, and load it through the Import Foreign Exchange Rates app. This is fully standard Public Cloud but manual/semi-manual (someone runs it each day). Reference: Import Foreign Exchange Rates – SAP Help Portal.

Summary

For a hands-off daily feed of MNB rates, use SAP Market Rates Management with Bring Your Own Rates: an Integration Suite iFlow reads MNB's SOAP service and POSTs to the MRM BYOR API, S/4HANA connects to MRM via SAP_COM_0203, and an Application Jobs schedule pulls the rates in daily — note this needs the MRM BTP subscription. If a subscription isn't available, fall back to the Import Foreign Exchange Rates app with a prepared MNB file. There is no standard "MNB → S/4HANA" direct API; the integration effort sits on your side up to the MRM/BYOR boundary.

Hope it helps.
Best regards,
Michal

entry.title: Re: What's new in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2608 release
entry.content:
Hi Bert,

Thank you for another excellent release overview. These blogs are always a great way to quickly identify the innovations that bring real value to customers.

One enhancement that immediately caught my attention is "Supporting product variant configuration data maintenance via Product Master API". I am particularly excited to see that the Product Master API will now support the creation and update of AVC (Advanced Variant Configuration) data at both header and plant level.

This is a capability I have been waiting for for quite some time, as I currently have a customer requirement where maintaining AVC data through modern REST APIs would be extremely valuable.

Unfortunately, I was unable to access the documentation through the link provided in the slide, and I also could not find the relevant API information in the SAP API Business Hub.

Would you be able to share the correct documentation and, if available, point me to the API details or API Hub entry for this enhancement?

Best regards,
Randy Timmers

entry.title: Re: Not able to see APIM in integration suite
entry.content:
Hi @Sam000 ,

Please configure the API Management service in your Integration Suite by following these steps:

  1. In the SAP BTP Cockpit, navigate to Security > Users, select your user, and assign the following role collections:
    1. APIManagement.Selfservice.Administrator – required to configure the API Management service
    2. AuthGroup.SelfService.Admin – required for the Developer Hub onboarding and access

  2. In Integration Suite, go to Settings > Runtimes and start the API Management setup
  3. During the configuration:
    1. Select the appropriate account type (Production or Non-Production)
    2. Enter a Host Alias (To-be URL will be: https://<virtualHost>.apimanagement.hana.ondemand.com)
    3. Provide a Notification Contact email address
    4. Choose Activate
    5. Review the configuration in the pop-up and choose Confirm to start the onboarding process

  4. Wait until the setup and validation complete successfully (all status indicators should turn green)
  5. Log out of Integration Suite and sign in again. API Management Service is now configured. You can now create APIs, build API proxies as a service provider, or use APIs and other convenient services

If you need further help and details please check our official sources:

  • Set Up API Management from Integration Suite
  • Setting Up API Management Help Page

entry.title: AI-Driven SAP Performance Insights
entry.content:
All my fellow SAP Basis admins know how useful the HANA Expensive Statements view in DBACOCKPIT can be.

It gives us detailed insight into performance-critical information like execution duration, CPU usage, memory consumption, application source, work process details, and much more. It is one of those views we often rely on when troubleshooting database performance issues.

But there is a challenge we all run into: the data does not stay around for long.

In a typical SAP S/4HANA system, expensive statement history is usually short-lived — often only around 6–8 hours in practical scenarios . So if an issue is reported later in the day, the next morning, or after a month-end process, the most useful evidence may already be gone.

That raises important questions:

 

  • When did the issue actually start?
  • How did the query perform yesterday or during month-end close?
  • Which users executed it in the past month?
  • What parameters made it expensive compared with normal executions?

 

With everything AI can do today, I started thinking: why not use AI to overcome the limitation of short-lived HANA expensive statement data — and make the analysis easier for both technical and functional teams?

That idea led to the HANA Expensive Statement Analyzer, an application designed to convert short-lived HANA expensive statement data into historical, searchable, natural-language insights

Solution Overview

The idea behind this solution is simple: capture the expensive statement data before it disappears, store it historically, and then use AI to make that data easier to analyze.

At a high level, the solution is built in three layers:

Layer 1: Data Collection and Persistence

An ABAP program periodically reads data from the HANA expensive statements source i.e. M_EXPENSIVE_STATEMENTS, and stores the relevant details in a custom SAP table.

A CDS view is then built on top of this table to structure and expose the historical data in a reusable way. This creates the foundation for analyzing expensive statements beyond the short retention window normally available in DBACOCKPIT.

Layer 2: API Exposure

The CDS-based data model is exposed through OData and made available as an API using SAP BTP AppRouter.

This layer helps securely expose the historical expensive statement data so it can be consumed by downstream applications or AI-based tools.

Layer 3: AI-Powered Natural Language Analysis

The API is then connected to an enterprise AI assistant, with a system prompt designed to help users ask meaningful performance-related questions in natural language.

For example, instead of manually filtering through historical records, users can ask questions like:

 

  • What were the top expensive statements today?
  • When did this query first become expensive?
  • Which time window had the highest database load last week?
  • Compare normal versus expensive executions for this statement.
  • Were there any expensive queries during month-end close?
  • Summarize performance issues for the last 30 days in business terms.

 

This makes the solution useful not only for SAP Basis administrators, but also for SAP developers and functional teams. Administrators can troubleshoot using current and historical data, developers can review expensive queries without recreating the issue, and business teams can ask performance questions in plain language.

A Look at the Tool in Action

Let’s take a quick look at how the tool works and how it can be useful for different personas — SAP Administrators, Developers, and Functional teams.

Below are a few sample prompts that show how each persona can use the tool in day-to-day performance analysis.

SAP Administrators

In the example below, I asked the tool to list the top 5 SQL statements by memory consumption for the day.

Along with returning the top 5 statements, the tool also provides useful observations such as:

 

  • Which statements consumed the most memory
  • Whether the statements belong to the same functional area
  • Whether the same statement hash appeared multiple times
  • Which user or application pattern may need further investigation
  • Whether the issue looks like a repeated expensive query pattern

 

This gives SAP Administrators a quick starting point for troubleshooting. Instead of only looking at raw expensive statement records, they can immediately see summarized insights that help identify users, execution times, application sources, peak load windows, and possible patterns for deeper analysis .

Sample prompt:

What are the top 5 SQLs by memory todayrsonal Favorite: Finding Busy and Idle Windows

 

One of my favorite examples is asking the tool to analyze a longer period of performance data and identify system usage trends.

Instead of looking at one expensive statement at a time, I can ask a broader question like:

Based on performance data for July, considering both memory and CPU, can you build a trend of the busiest times and idle times in the system?

This is where the historical data becomes really powerful. Since the expensive statement data is captured and stored over time, the tool can analyze patterns across days, weeks, or business cycles instead of being limited to the short-lived DBACOCKPIT history.

These are just a couple of examples, but the opportunities are endless. By combining historical expensive statement data with AI-powered natural-language analysis, SAP Administrators can quickly identify patterns, peak load windows, recurring expensive queries, and areas that need deeper investigation

SAP Developers

SAP Developers can use the tool to quickly identify expensive SQL statements originating from custom programs, CDS views, or application sources. Instead of waiting to recreate the issue or collect new traces, developers can analyze historical executions, compare expensive versus normal runs, and identify recurring patterns that may point to optimization opportunities.

For example, a developer can ask:

Can you list the top 5 memory-consuming statements for June 2026 originating from Z/custom application sources?

The tool can then summarize the most expensive custom/Z-originated statements, highlight memory and CPU usage, show repeated statement hashes, and provide key observations that help developers decide where to focus tuning efforts.

This helps developers move faster from “which query is expensive?” to “what custom object or pattern should I investigate and optimize?

 

SAP Functional Teams

Functional teams can use the tool as a self-service capability to get quick insights into expensive queries related to their business areas. Instead of waiting for detailed technical analysis, they can ask performance questions in natural language and understand whether a performance issue may be related to user selections, business process volume, reporting filters, or other functional factors within their purview .

By reviewing the parameters passed to a query, functional teams can often identify whether the issue is caused by broad selections, missing filters, large date ranges, high-volume business scenarios, or unusual usage patterns. This helps them validate the business context before engaging SAP Administrators or Developers for deeper technical troubleshooting.

For example, a functional user can ask:

Based on July performance data, list the top 5 expensive queries by memory in the finance area.

The tool can summarize the most expensive queries for that business area, highlight related objects, and provide tuning-focused observations that can help functional teams collaborate more effectively with Basis and development teams.

This makes performance analysis more accessible beyond technical teams and helps move troubleshooting from “technical data only” to a shared conversation between functional, Basis, and development teams.

 

Conclusion

AI gives SAP Administrators a powerful opportunity to simplify routine analysis, reduce manual effort, and focus more on complex performance challenges. By combining historical HANA expensive statement data with natural-language analysis, we can move beyond short-lived monitoring and turn technical performance data into actionable insights.

This helps SAP Administrators, Developers, and Functional teams troubleshoot faster, identify recurring patterns, and collaborate more effectively when resolving performance issues.

Using AI in the right way allows us to add more value to the business — solving complex problems with better context, faster turnaround time, and more meaningful insights.

Please share your feedback, and feel free to comment if you have any technical questions about the solution. I would be happy to exchange ideas and learn from others working on similar SAP performance challenges.

entry.title: Governing the End-to-End Service Cycle in SAP S/4HANA Service with SAP Joule Agents
entry.content:
Introduction

Service is one of the most demanding business processes in SAP S/4HANA.

Every customer interaction can turn into a Service Request, a Service Quotation, a Service Order, and eventually a Service Confirmation — each with its own mandatory data, its own upstream dependency, and its own point at which mistakes become expensive.

Even with modern Fiori experiences, service teams still spend a large part of their day navigating between apps, resolving Business Partners, checking statuses, and validating whether upstream documents are actually ready. When something is missed, the cost is not just rework — it is billing errors, compliance gaps, and eroded customer trust.

With the arrival of SAP Joule, SAP Joule Studio, and the Generative AI Hub in SAP AI Core, we now have a governed way to bring intelligent, conversational experiences directly into these processes — without stepping outside SAP's business rules.

This blog walks through three tightly integrated SAP Joule Agents that together govern the complete service cycle in SAP S/4HANA — a Service Intake Assistant, a Service Order Assistant, and a Service Confirmation Assistant — and shares the design principles and lessons learned from building them.

If you are new to SAP Joule Studio or the Generative AI Hub, the SAP resources linked at the end of this blog are a good place to start before diving in.

The Business Challenge:

Service organizations across Industrial Equipment, Engineering Services, Field Service, and Utilities consistently report the same pain points across the service cycle:

  • Manual navigation and fragmented validation during intake
  • Ambiguous Business Partner resolution leading to downstream billing issues
  • Service Orders created without valid upstream commercial references
  • Premature order releases and uncontrolled updates
  • Planned values reused as actuals during confirmation
  • Irreversible service closure executed without readiness checks
  • Missing audit trail across intake, order, and confirmation stages

The net result: slower cycle times, higher operational risk, weaker first-time-right rates, and financial data that Finance and Analytics cannot fully trust.

What service teams need is not another chatbot — it is a governed, conversational execution layer that respects SAP's business rules at every step of the cycle.

The Three Service Management Agents:

Three SAP Joule Agents, one governed lifecycle.

Figure 1 — End-to-end Service Management cycle governed by the three SAP Joule Agents: Service Intake Assistant, Service Order Assistant, and Service Confirmation Assistant. Every stage follows the same pattern — natural language in, governed validation, backend-confirmed outcome.

1. Service Intake Assistant:

The starting point of the cycle. This agent delivers a governed, AI-assisted intake experience for Service Requests and Service Quotations. It resolves Business Partners from SAP master data, validates mandatory intake information in a single consolidated step, and creates or updates service documents only after successful SAP backend execution — ensuring first-time-right intake and eliminating incomplete service documents at the source.

Because the Service Intake Assistant is the entry point to the entire service cycle, the following walkthrough illustrates its behavior in depth. The same conversational, consolidated-validation, backend-confirmed pattern is then applied consistently by the Service Order Assistant and the Service Confirmation Assistant across the rest of the lifecycle.

 

 

Screenshot 1 — Launching the Service Intake Assistant inside SAP Joule for a natural-language conversation with a service user.

The interaction begins with a simple natural-language request from the service user. The agent then resolves the Business Partner directly against SAP master data and retrieves the partner's existing service documents before creating anything new.

Screenshot 2 — The Service Intake Assistant resolves the Business Partner against SAP master data and retrieves all existing Service Requests for the partner before any new document is created.

Once the Business Partner is confirmed, the agent gathers every mandatory field in a single consolidated interaction rather than a long back-and-forth. If anything is missing, ambiguous, or blocking, the agent communicates it transparently along with what has already been collected — nothing is assumed, nothing is fabricated.

 

Screenshot 3 — Consolidated single-step validation in action: the agent displays every field already available and transparently flags the single missing mandatory input before proceeding to backend execution.

Only when all validations pass does the agent invoke the corresponding SAP backend operation. Success is confirmed only after SAP S/4HANA returns a valid document number — never before.

 

 

Screenshot 4 — Backend-confirmed Service Request creation in SAP S/4HANA Service Management — the agent confirms success only after the SAP backend returns a valid document number.

The same governed pattern applies to Service Quotations, which the agent creates against the confirmed Business Partner with full pricing, quantity, validity, and status returned directly from SAP.

 

Screenshot 5 — Backend-confirmed Service Quotation creation for the same Business Partner, with pricing, quantity, validity, and status returned directly from SAP S/4HANA.

Updates to existing service documents follow the same discipline. The agent applies the change only after backend confirmation and presents a clear before-and-after view so that the update is auditable.

 

Screenshot 6 — Controlled update of an existing Service Quotation, with the agent showing the previous and new values only after backend confirmation of the update. 

2. Service Order Assistant:

The execution governance layer of the cycle. This agent controls Service Order creation, updates, and release. It ensures that Service Orders are created only from valid upstream Service Quotation references, enforces consolidated dependency validation, and executes release only when SAP backend conditions are met — eliminating unauthorized or orphan Service Orders and preventing premature releases.

The following walkthrough illustrates the Service Order lifecycle end-to-end: retrieve, create, update, and release.

 

Screenshot 7 — Launching the Service Order Assistant inside SAP Joule, which supports the complete Service Order lifecycle: retrieve, create, update, and release.

Before any Service Order is created, the agent enforces its most important governance rule — the presence of a valid upstream Service Quotation reference. Only after the Quotation is confirmed does the agent request the mandatory fields for Service Order creation.

 

Screenshot 8 — Upstream dependency enforcement: the Service Order Assistant validates the referenced Service Quotation before collecting the mandatory fields required for Service Order creation.

When all validations pass, the agent executes the create operation and confirms success only after SAP S/4HANA returns a valid Service Order number, linked back to the originating Quotation for traceability.

 

Screenshot 9 — Backend-confirmed Service Order creation, linked to the upstream Service Quotation. Success is confirmed only after SAP S/4HANA returns a valid Service Order number.

Updates to existing Service Orders follow the same discipline. The agent applies changes only after backend confirmation and presents a clear before-and-after view for auditability.

 

Screenshot 10 — Controlled item-level update of the Service Order, with the agent showing previous and new values only after successful backend confirmation.

Release is treated as a distinct governance event. Before any release is executed, the agent runs a full Release Readiness Assessment — verifying every prerequisite and reporting blockers transparently. Release is permitted only when the assessment returns clean.

 

Screenshot 11 — Release Readiness Assessment: the Service Order Assistant verifies every release prerequisite (existence, status, upstream reference, organizational data, service details) and reports "no blockers detected" before any release is executed.

Once released, the Service Order is officially available for work scheduling and execution — which is the natural handoff cue to the Service Confirmation Assistant, the third agent in the cycle.

 

Screenshot 12 — Backend-confirmed release of the Service Order in SAP S/4HANA. The agent confirms release status, pricing document creation, and execution readiness only after successful backend validation — completing the governed handoff into service execution.

3. Service Confirmation Assistant:

The closure layer of the cycle. This agent governs actuals-based Service Confirmation creation, updates, and final completion. It ensures that confirmations are recorded only against valid, released Service Orders, enforces a clear separation between planned and actual execution data, and performs final completion only after SAP backend confirmation — treating closure as the irreversible business action it is.

The following walkthrough illustrates the complete Service Confirmation lifecycle: retrieve, create, update, and complete.

 

Screenshot 13 — Launching the Service Confirmation Assistant inside SAP Joule. The agent supports the complete Service Confirmation lifecycle: retrieve, create, update, and — as an explicitly irreversible action — complete.

Before any confirmation is created, the agent verifies the upstream Service Order's release status and reports whether confirmations already exist. This is the discipline that keeps actuals aligned to real, released work.

 

Screenshot 14 — Before any confirmation is created, the agent retrieves the upstream Service Order's release status and reports the existing confirmation state — enforcing the rule that actuals can only be recorded against valid, released Service Orders.

Once readiness is confirmed, the agent gathers every mandatory field in a single consolidated request — the same design pattern applied by the Intake and Order Assistants.

 

Screenshot 15 — Consolidated mandatory field collection for Service Confirmation creation — the same single-step validation pattern applied by every agent in the Service Management set.

The agent then executes the create operation and confirms success only after SAP S/4HANA returns a valid Service Confirmation ID, linked back to the released Service Order for full traceability.

 

Screenshot 16 — Backend-confirmed Service Confirmation creation in SAP S/4HANA, linked back to the released Service Order. Success is confirmed only after the SAP backend returns a valid Service Confirmation ID.

Updates continue to follow the same auditable discipline as the previous agents in the cycle — with a clean before-and-after view after every backend-confirmed change.

 

Screenshot 17 — Controlled update of an existing Service Confirmation, with the agent presenting previous and new values only after backend confirmation — the same auditability pattern applied across the cycle.

Finally, completion is treated as the irreversible business action that it is. The agent captures actual service duration and start time, performs final completion only after SAP backend confirmation, and explicitly communicates that the confirmation is finalized and cannot be undone.

Screenshot 18 — Irreversible Service Confirmation completion in SAP S/4HANA. The agent records actual service duration and start time, finalizes the confirmation as an audit-ready, non-reversible business action, and closes the end-to-end service cycle that began with the Service Intake Assistant.

How the Agents Work Together:

The Service Intake walkthrough above illustrated a design pattern that all three agents share: natural language in, governed SAP execution out.

The Service Order Assistant applies the same discipline to Service Order creation, updates, and release — refusing to create orders without a valid upstream Service Quotation reference, enforcing consolidated dependency validation, and releasing only when SAP backend conditions are met.

The Service Confirmation Assistant closes the loop with the same rigor — recording actual only against released Service Orders, separating planned and actual data cleanly, and performing final closure only after SAP backend confirmation.

The result is a service lifecycle in which no stage can be skipped, and no stage can be executed without the previous one being genuinely valid — exactly what enterprise-grade service governance requires.

 

Figure 2 — Shared design pattern across all three agents: user intent expressed in natural language, consolidated context and validation, governed SAP backend execution, and confirmed outcome that hands off cleanly to the next stage of the service lifecycle.

Solution Architecture:

All three agents are built entirely on SAP's AI and application stack.

Figure 3 — Technical realization on SAP Business Technology Platform: SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Build Process Automation, SAP Joule Studio, SAP AI Core, and SAP Cloud Identity Services deployed in a multi-cloud subaccount, connected to on-premises SAP S/4HANA or SAP ECC through Cloud Connector via OData/REST and HTTPS.

Architecture Components:

  • SAP Joule — Conversational interaction layer for service users.
  • SAP Joule Studio — Design, configure, and orchestrate each agent, its skills, guardrails, and prompts.
  • SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) — Runtime, identity, and extensibility foundation shared across the agent set.
  • Generative AI Hub in SAP AI Core — Governed access to large language models, prompt orchestration, and AI governance.
  • SAP S/4HANA Service Management — System of record for Business Partners, Service Requests, Service Quotations, Service Orders, and Service Confirmations.

Business Value:

Adopting the three Service Management agents together delivers value that no single-point automation can match:

  • First-time-right service intake — Governed data collection and Business Partner resolution eliminate incomplete Service Requests and Quotations at the source.
  • Controlled, traceable execution — Every Service Order is created and released from a valid upstream reference, preventing unauthorized or orphan orders.
  • Accurate, irreversible closure — Confirmations capture real actuals against released orders, protecting billing accuracy and audit readiness.
  • Faster cycle times — Removing manual navigation and fragmented validation shortens intake, execution, and closure across the lifecycle.
  • Trustworthy service data — Finance and Analytics receive service data that reflects real backend execution, not optimistic UI states.
  • Reduced operational risk — Governance is enforced by design across every stage, not left to individual user discipline.

Example Use Cases:

  • A Service Desk Operator captures a customer request and creates a Service Request through a natural-language conversation, without navigating any Fiori app.
  • A Service Order Coordinator converts an approved Service Quotation into a controlled Service Order and releases it once all dependencies are satisfied.
  • A Field Service Technician records actual time and effort against a released Service Order and closes the service as a final, irreversible action.
  • A Customer Service Manager retrieves and reviews open Service Quotations, Service Orders, or Service Confirmations for a specific Business Partner through a single conversational query.
  • An Operations Lead uses the same conversational surface across all three agents to move a service case cleanly from request to closure.

Lessons Learned Building an End-to-End Agent Set:

Building not one but three interlinked SAP Joule Agents surfaced lessons that are directly relevant to anyone building on SAP Joule Studio and the Generative AI Hub.

  1. Design for the Cycle, Not for the Agent

The single biggest architectural decision was to design the three agents as a coherent set rather than three isolated agents. Handoffs between intake, order, and confirmation are where enterprise value is created — and where governance is most easily lost.

  1. Governance Is Non-Negotiable at Every Stage

Every stage of the service cycle has its own point at which mistakes become expensive. The agents therefore enforce validation, upstream references, and backend confirmation at every stage, not just at the point of creation.

  1. Consolidated Validation Beats Iterative Prompting

Users abandon agents that ask for information one field at a time. Collecting and validating in a single consolidated step turned out to be one of the highest-impact UX decisions across all three agents.

  1. Never Confirm Before the Backend Confirms

Optimistic confirmations erode trust immediately. Every agent in the set returns success only when SAP S/4HANA returns a valid outcome — even when that makes the conversation slightly longer.

  1. Actuals Deserve the Same Rigor as Creation

Service Confirmations are the stage where planned and actual data are most often confused. Treating actuals capture and final closure with the same rigor as intake and order creation was essential to protect billing accuracy and audit readiness.

  1. A Shared Design Language Accelerates Everything

Because all three agents follow the same natural-language-in, backend-confirmed-out pattern, users learn one interaction model and reuse it across the entire cycle — which is a significant adoption advantage.

Conclusion:

The Service Intake Assistant, Service Order Assistant, and Service Confirmation Assistant together demonstrate how SAP Business AI, SAP Joule, SAP Joule Studio, and the SAP Generative AI Hub can be used to govern an entire enterprise business cycle — not just automate a single step.

The three agents show that agentic AI on SAP does not have to trade off governance for productivity. When designed as a coherent set, SAP Joule Agents can deliver faster execution, higher data quality, controlled handoffs, and audit-ready outcomes across the full service cycle.

As enterprises accelerate their SAP Business AI journeys, an end-to-end agent set like this represents a practical, production-ready path to governed, conversational service execution.

If you are exploring SAP Joule Studio or building your first SAP Joule Agent, I would be happy to hear how you are approaching governance, consolidated validation, and backend confirmation in your own designs.

Please share your thoughts in the comments below.

About the Author:

The author is a Lead Architect working on SAP Business AI, SAP Joule, and SAP BTP, focused on the design and delivery of governed SAP Joule Agents for enterprise business processes on SAP S/4HANA.

entry.title: Re: CDS view to get the PO Workflow data
entry.content: Released CDS VIEWS I_WorkflowStatusDetails - Top level work item status I_WorkflowStatusOverview - Current work item READY in inbox I_WorkflowRecipients - Shows the user to approve the work item I_WorkflowTaskExternalStatus - all availabel statuses I_WorkflowExternalStatus - all available statuses I_WorkflowExternalStatusText - All status texts in each language None of these views expose the child/step-level work item data (the individual READY and PLANNED rows) that you can see in SWWWIHEAD table. There is no standard released CDS view that surfaces the individual step-level work item statuses (e.g., READY, PLANNED) stored in that table https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/whitelisted-cds-view-to-get-approval-details-of-purchase-requisition/qaq-p/12335509 https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-resource-planning-q-a/custom-report-to-provide-approval-dates-for-each-step-of-workflow/qaq-p/12324435
